title: practical english usage - Part 98
content:
Police, staff and crew are generally used in the same way.

At one time many cattle suffered from a disease called BSE.

say, for instance, three cattle).

has no singular, and cannot be used for counting individual animals (one cannot Cattle is a plural word used to talk collectively about bulls, cows and calves; it 7 plurals     with    no   singular      forms the meaning is plural (e.g. shoe shop). There are some exceptions. (►   125.4).

In                      combinations, the first noun is usually singular in form even if (less formal).

court or military trial) is either courts martial (more formal) or court martials but some people use mother-in-laws, etc. The  plural of court martial (= military The  plural of mother-in-law and similar words is generally mothers-in-law, etc, runner-up     runners-up passer-by     passers-by Singular                               Plural In noun + adverb combinations, the plural -s is usually added to the noun.

6 compound        nouns It is not correct to use -'s in other plurals, e.g. jean's.

PC's are getting cheaper. (PCs is more common) I loved the 1990's. (the 1990s is more common) She wrote 'necessary' with two c's.

alphabet, and sometimes in the plurals of dates and abbreviations.

An apostrophe (’ ) can be used before the -s in the plurals of letters of the 5 plurals     in  's (►   119.5).

Note that some foreign plurals (e.g. agenda, spaghetti) are singular in English vertebra  vertebrae (Latin) stimulus              stimuli (Latin) radius                  radii (Latin) phenomenon       phenomena (Greek) oasis                    oases (Greek) nucleus                nuclei (Latin) kibbutz                kibbutzim (Hebrew) hypothesis           hypotheses (Greek) or fungus                  fungi (Latin)     funguses or formula               formulae (Latin)     formulas diagnosis             diagnoses (Greek) criterion               criteria (Greek) crisis                    crises (Greek) Singular                             Plural Nouns and Noun Phrases; Agreement Section     11 grammar *118   pronunciation of plurals knives /naɪvz/ trees /tri:z/                     hills /hɪlz/                            songs /snŋz/ boys /bɔɪz/                             ends /endz/                       dreams /driɪmz/ days /deiz/                         clothes /kləuðz/                     legs /legz/ -(e)s is pronounced /z/.

After vowels, and all voiced consonants except /z/, /ʒ/   and /dʒ/,   the plural ending 3  nouns     ending     in  other     voiced     sounds beliefs /bɪ'li:fs/                   plates /pleɪts/ cups /kʌps/                         cloths /kloθs/                       books /boks/ is pronounced /s/.

After any other unvoiced sound (/p/, /f/, /θ/,  /t/ or /k/), the plural ending -(e)s 2 nouns     ending     in  other     unvoiced       sounds quizzes /'kwɪzɪz/                  garages /'gærɑ:ʒɪz/                  bridges ^bndʒɪz/ buses /'bʌsɪz/                      crashes /'kræʃiz/                  watches /'wDtfiz/ is pronounced /iz/.

After one of the sibilant sounds /s/, /z/, /ʃ/,  /ʒ/,  /tʃ/ and /dʒ/,   the plural ending -es 1  nouns     ending     in  /s/, /z/ and   other     sibilants 118  pronunciation          of  plurals For cases where plural nouns are used with singular verbs and pronouns (and the opposite), ►     128-129.

She lives on the outskirts of Cambridge, (     . . .-the outskirt...) not Congratulations on your new job. (     Congratulation ...) supplies, surroundings, thanks, troops, valuables, wages.

outskirts, premises (= building), proceeds, refreshments, regards, remains, savings, barley and rye are singular uncountable), odds (= chances), odds-and-ends, (= social behaviour), the Middle Ages (a period in history), oats (but corn, wheat, frontier), earnings, funds (= money), goods, groceries, leftovers, lodgings, manners arms (= guns, etc), clothes (►  423), congratulations, contents, customs (at a include: Other common words which  are normally plural and don't have singular forms Have you got a pair of nail scissors?
